tldr: Headlines frame Hormuz simultaneously as an active military-economic chokepoint and a collapsing diplomatic negotiation: ship seizures and submarine deployments dominate the security register while Trump-Xi alignment on keeping the strait open provides the sole visible area of agreement, and ceasefire collapse is the political throughline.

evidence.quote: Ship is reported seized off the coast of the UAE
Iran regime has 'gun to our head' over Strait of Hormuz
Iran says its small subs deployed to Strait of Hormuz
Iran Must Only Succeed Once to Trigger a Catastrophe
Trump, Xi agree on need for Hormuz to stay open
Blockade of Strait of Hormuz not in common interests
It's In China's Interest To Pressure Iran To Open Strait Of Hormuz
Iran sets tough conditions for US talks amid mounting fears
Hormuz disruption persists, trade costs rise
Hormuz blockade and the fracturing of Asia's growth narrative
'Inevitable' jet fuel shortages will drive up air fares this summer
Why Iran war is driving up ink prices
Trump says Iran ceasefire 'on life support' as deal hopes fade
Iran dares Trump to reject its 'take it or leave it' deal
Trump faces split among retired US commanders
Iran analysts pessimistic on US talks despite China's role

isolation_top.note: Headline covers a single crude tanker completing a rare successful Hormuz transit — logistics frame semantically distant from the geopolitical/ceasefire corpus
Estonia's potential Hormuz mission participation — capacity-planning framing at the margins of the story, narrow bureaucratic angle
Single headline on Australia joining the maritime security mission — adjacent-actor angle, not core Hormuz-Iran diplomacy
RT Africa files frame Iran war through piracy and Nigerian oil, peripheral angles disconnected from the strait-closure and ceasefire corpus
Atlantic photo essay on global human cost and Danish-play analogy — long-form analytical registers that land far from news-wire framing
CGTN headlines are notably restrained and procedural (Wang Yi statement, UKMTO access restrictions) — factual-diplomatic register distinct from alarm-heavy corpus

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Iran's official media position on Hormuz — sovereign rights framing, ceasefire conditions — is entirely absent; Iran appears only through opposition media and adversarial Western framing
France led the European maritime mission to Hormuz but filed nothing in this corpus; European security-alliance dimension unrepresented except through Poland/Baltic
UAE, Saudi Arabia and Bahrain — the states most directly exposed to Hormuz closure and the ship seizure near UAE waters — are absent despite being the primary economic victims

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet leading with a successful Hormuz crossing as a news event — frames rare transit itself as the story, inverting the blockade narrative
Only outlet leading with a downstream consumer-price curiosity (Japanese ink shortage) — the most lateral economic-impact angle in the entire corpus
Only headline quoting a military expert making an explicit asymmetric-risk case ('Iran must only succeed once') — frames the strait as inherently vulnerable rather than contested
Only outlet framing the Iran war primarily through photographic documentation of global human cost — humanitarian register absent from all hard-news titles

bottom_line: Headline coverage of Hormuz/Iran is dominated by military-threat and ceasefire-collapse framing, with economic contagion headlines a distant second and US-China summit alignment as the sole diplomatic signal in a corpus otherwise saturated with failure.
